text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
INTERVAL SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

Patient: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
Diagnosis: High-grade serous carcinoma, bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0)
Germline Status: BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, 5382insC)
Current Therapy: Niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily (maintenance, dose-reduced)
Most Recent Imaging: Surveillance CT C/A/P with contrast approximately 6 months prior — no evidence of disease recurrence

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 48-to-49-year-old woman with a history of FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, currently on niraparib maintenance, presenting for a scheduled surveillance visit. She endorses a new symptom of mild right-sided flank discomfort present over the past two weeks, which prompted expedited evaluation today in addition to her routine labs.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is now approximately 30 months from her initial diagnosis of high-grade serous carcinoma of the bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC. She underwent successful primary debulking surgery (TAH-BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, peritoneal stripping including diaphragm) achieving complete gross resection (R0) at initial surgery. She subsequently completed 6 cycles of adjuvant carboplatin AUC 5 / paclitaxel (dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 for the last two cycles due to cumulative peripheral neuropathy), achieving a complete biochemical and radiographic response with CA-125 normalization and no evidence of disease on restaging CT. She has been on niraparib maintenance since approximately 8 months after diagnosis, currently ongoing at 100 mg daily (dose-reduced from the initial 200 mg due to Grade 3 thrombocytopenia in the first month of therapy; restarted at 100 mg with subsequent Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia that has been stable and manageable with monthly CBC monitoring). She is now at approximately 22 months on niraparib at the 100 mg dose.

With respect to her chief complaint today: over the past two weeks, she has noted a dull, intermittent aching discomfort in the right flank, which she describes as a 3-4/10 in intensity. It is not colicky in nature but does occasionally radiate toward the right lower quadrant and groin. There is no associated fever, chills, dysuria, frequency beyond her usual baseline, or gross hematuria that she has noticed. She has not had nausea or vomiting in relation to the flank pain. She denies any new abdominal bloating or distension — she is acutely attuned to abdominal symptoms given her history and specifically states that this flank discomfort feels different from her original ovarian cancer presentation. She has been drinking approximately 6-8 glasses of water daily and has not noted any change in urine output or color.

She denies new pelvic pressure, any palpable abdominal mass, or change in bowel habits. No night sweats, no significant unintentional weight change. No bone pain. No shortness of breath.

She is currently tolerating niraparib 100 mg daily reasonably well. She takes it at bedtime to minimize nausea. She continues on amlodipine 10 mg daily for niraparib-related hypertension, which has been well-controlled at recent checks. She continues duloxetine 30 mg daily for residual peripheral neuropathy, which has been slowly improving.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, significant weight loss (weight stable at approximately 63-64 kg). Reports mild baseline fatigue (Grade 1), which she attributes to niraparib; manageable and not limiting her activities.
Neurologic: Persistent mild tingling in bilateral feet (Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy, residual from paclitaxel); stable and not limiting. Headaches resolved since blood pressure optimization.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no leg swelling. Blood pressure well-controlled on amlodipine.
Pulmonary: No cough, no dyspnea, no pleuritic pain.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea (mild, Grade 1, managed with PRN antiemetics when needed). No vomiting. Normal bowel habits. No rectal bleeding. No abdominal distension or bloating.
Genitourinary: Right flank discomfort as above; trace hematuria on dipstick (found on urinalysis today). No gross hematuria, no dysuria, no urinary frequency beyond baseline, no urinary urgency.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain, no joint pain.
Dermatologic: No new rashes. Hair fully regrown post-chemotherapy.
Endocrine: No hot flashes reported currently; patient declined hormone replacement therapy at time of surgery. Mild menopausal symptoms largely resolved.
Psychosocial: Reports good emotional wellbeing. Continues to attend the gynecologic oncology support group monthly. Back to full-time teaching. Daughter (age 22) recently found to be a BRCA1 carrier on genetic testing, which has caused some emotional distress for the patient; referred back to genetic counseling.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 126/80 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm, temperature 37.0°C, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight: 63.8 kg.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Appears her stated age. Alert and oriented.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No supraclavicular or cervical lymphadenopathy palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No pleural rubs.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Midline vertical laparotomy scar well-healed and non-tender. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses. No ascites by examination (no fluid wave, no shifting dullness). Mild right costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness to fist percussion — not severe, reproducible. No left CVA tenderness. No guarding or rigidity.

Pelvis (limited external): Post-hysterectomy/BSO status. Vault palpation not performed at this visit given no pelvic symptomatology.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness.

Neurologic: Mild diminished sensation to light touch in bilateral toes, symmetric, consistent with chronic Grade 1 paclitaxel-related neuropathy. No motor deficits.

Skin: No petechiae, no purpura, no ecchymoses.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CA-125: 10 U/mL (normal; reference <35 U/mL). Persistently in the low-normal range, consistent with sustained complete biochemical response.
HE4: Not drawn at this visit (last checked and normal at post-treatment assessment).

CBC:
  WBC: 5.0 x10^9/L (normal)
  ANC: 2.8 x10^9/L (normal)
  Hemoglobin: 11.9 g/dL (mild, Grade 1 anemia; stable and chronic on niraparib)
  Platelets: 118,000/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia; stable at this level for multiple months on niraparib 100 mg)
  MCV: 89 fL (normocytic)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride: 102 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
  BUN: 14 mg/dL, Creatinine: 0.81 mg/dL (stable, eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2)
  Glucose: 92 mg/dL
  AST: 22 U/L, ALT: 18 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase: 64 U/L, Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
  Total protein and albumin: within normal limits

Urinalysis (dipstick): Color: pale yellow, clarity: clear. Specific gravity: 1.012. pH: 6.0. Protein: negative. Glucose: negative. Ketones: negative. Blood: 1+ (trace hematuria). Leukocyte esterase: negative. Nitrite: negative. No casts on microscopy. RBCs: 5-10 per high-power field; no WBCs, no bacteria on spun sediment.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Urogram (performed today, preliminary read reviewed; formal report to follow): 4 mm non-obstructing calculus identified in the right lower pole calyx. No hydronephrosis, no hydroureter. No upper tract urothelial abnormality. Bilateral kidneys normal in size and parenchymal enhancement. Post-surgical pelvic changes from prior hysterectomy/BSO stable and unremarkable. No pelvic masses, no ascites, no peritoneal implants identified on this study. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Prior surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (approximately 6 months ago, at 24 months from initial diagnosis): No evidence of disease recurrence; complete and sustained radiographic remission.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, FIGO IIIC — IN SUSTAINED COMPLETE REMISSION

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is now 30 months from her diagnosis of stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma. She achieved an outstanding initial response to primary debulking surgery (R0) and adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel, with complete biochemical and radiographic remission that has been sustained throughout her niraparib maintenance. Her CA-125 today is 10 U/mL — persistently in the low-normal range for many months. There is no clinical, biochemical, or imaging evidence of disease recurrence at this time.

The new right flank symptom, while initially prompting concern, has been thoroughly investigated today. The CT urogram shows a small 4 mm non-obstructing right lower pole renal calculus as the source of her flank discomfort and trace hematuria. Importantly, the pelvic and abdominal survey on the CT urogram shows no evidence of recurrent disease — no peritoneal implants, no masses, no ascites, no lymphadenopathy. This is very reassuring. I have explained to the patient that her symptoms are not consistent with cancer recurrence, and that the stone is a common and benign finding.

Plan:
- Continue niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily at the current well-tolerated dose. Patient is approximately 22 months into niraparib maintenance. Plan for total duration of approximately 36 months from initiation per SOLO-1 trial data supporting PARP inhibitor maintenance in germline BRCA1 patients with complete response to first-line platinum-based therapy.
- CA-125 every 3 months — next in approximately 3 months.
- Next formal surveillance CT C/A/P scheduled at 36 months from chemotherapy completion (approximately 6 months from today).

2. RIGHT RENAL CALCULUS — NEW

Trauma hematuria and right flank discomfort are attributable to a 4 mm non-obstructing right lower pole renal calculus on CT urogram. Patient hemodynamically stable, afebrile, no evidence of obstruction or infection. Stone size of 4 mm is favorable for spontaneous passage.

Plan:
- Conservative management: aggressive oral hydration (target 2.5-3 L of water daily).
- Tamsulosin (Flomax) 0.4 mg orally at bedtime initiated today (alpha-blocker to facilitate stone passage; discussed with patient that this is an off-label but well-established use).
- Urology consultation placed; referral sent today. Likely a straightforward outpatient evaluation, no emergent intervention anticipated given small stone size and absence of obstruction.
- Patient instructed to strain urine for stone passage and present to the emergency department if she develops worsening pain, fever, inability to tolerate fluids, or signs of obstruction.
- Follow-up with urology within 2-3 weeks.
- Niraparib is not a known cause of urolithiasis; no adjustment to niraparib therapy indicated.

3. NIRAPARIB-RELATED THROMBOCYTOPENIA — CHRONIC, STABLE

Platelet count today is 118,000/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia). This has been stable at the 100-120K range for several months at the dose-reduced 100 mg daily level. The patient has had no bleeding episodes. Per the prescribing information and clinical judgment, niraparib can be safely continued at this dose level with Grade 1 thrombocytopenia.

Plan:
- Continue niraparib 100 mg daily.
- Monthly CBC monitoring maintained.
- If platelets drop below 75,000/uL, hold niraparib and reassess per dose modification protocol.

4. NIRAPARIB-RELATED HYPERTENSION — CONTROLLED

Blood pressure today 126/80 mmHg, well-controlled on amlodipine 10 mg daily. No adjustment needed.

Plan:
- Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily.
- Continue blood pressure monitoring at monthly CBC visits.

5. PERIPHERAL SENSORY NEUROPATHY — RESIDUAL, GRADE 1, IMPROVING

Grade 1 residual paclitaxel-related peripheral neuropathy in bilateral feet (mild tingling in toes), slowly improving. No functional limitation. Duloxetine 30 mg daily continued.

Plan:
- Continue duloxetine 30 mg daily.
- Reassess neuropathy at each visit; consider duloxetine taper at a future visit if neuropathy continues to improve.

6. BRCA1 CARRIER — ONGOING SURVEILLANCE AND FAMILY IMPLICATIONS

Patient carries germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC). She has been counseled regarding breast cancer risk reduction strategies. She declined risk-reducing mastectomy at this time. Annual breast MRI and mammography in place for intensive surveillance.

Her daughter (age 22) was recently found to be a BRCA1 carrier. Patient has expressed some emotional distress regarding this. Discussed that her daughter has time to make thoughtful informed decisions with genetic counselors and breast/gynecologic oncology specialists, and that early identification is a meaningful advantage.

Plan:
- Confirm breast MRI surveillance is up to date (prior report at outside institution filed; no suspicious findings noted).
- Continue to encourage patient and family in genetic counseling engagement.
- Annual review of risk-reduction options per BRCA1 management guidelines.

---

MEDICATION REVIEW

- Niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily (taken at bedtime)
- Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily
- Duloxetine (Cymbalta) 30 mg orally once daily
- Ondansetron 8 mg orally PRN nausea (not actively using daily)
- Tamsulosin 0.4 mg orally at bedtime — NEW today
- Multivitamin daily

Allergies: Carboplatin (Grade 2 hypersensitivity reaction — facial flushing, urticaria, chest tightness; managed with desensitization protocol during chemotherapy; no further carboplatin exposures ongoing).

---

FOLLOW-UP

- Urology clinic: 2-3 weeks.
- Oncology follow-up: 3 months for CA-125 and CBC (sooner if stone symptoms worsen or new oncologic concerns).
- Surveillance CT C/A/P: approximately 6 months (at the 36-month post-chemotherapy mark).
- Breast surveillance MRI/mammography: as scheduled per outside radiology program.

Time spent with patient in direct counseling and coordination: approximately 40 minutes.

Electronically signed,
Attending Gynecologic Oncologist / Medical Oncologist
